subject: ap-psychology
title: Article Analysis Question (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This study examined whether a brief gratitude journaling intervention reduces symptoms of depression and increases life satisfaction in adults experiencing mild to moderate depressive symptoms.
Participants
Two hundred and twelve adults were recruited through online advertisements targeting individuals who scored in the mild-to-moderate range on a validated depression screening questionnaire (PHQ-9 scores 5–14). After screening, 198 participants were enrolled. Participants who reported a current diagnosis of major depressive disorder, bipolar disorder, or were currently receiving psychotherapy were excluded. The final sample included 98 women, 81 men, and 19 participants who identified as non-binary or gender-nonconforming. Ages ranged from 22 to 58 years (mean = 34.6, standard deviation = 7.3). The sample was 55% White/European American, 17% Black/African American, 14% Hispanic/Latino, 9% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other.
Method
Participants completed baseline measures of depression severity (PHQ-9) and life satisfaction (Satisfaction with Life Scale, SWLS) before being randomly assigned to one of two conditions for a four-week intervention period. In the gratitude journaling condition, participants received daily email reminders prompting them to write at least three things they were grateful for that day in an online journal. In the control condition, participants received daily email reminders prompting them to write a brief description of their activities earlier that day (e.g., what they ate for breakfast, what errands they ran). Journals were submitted online and compliance was monitored; participants who completed fewer than 20 of the 28 journal entries were excluded from final analysis, leaving a final analyzed sample of 174 participants (88 gratitude condition, 86 control).
At the end of four weeks, participants completed the PHQ-9 and SWLS again. Higher PHQ-9 scores indicate more severe depression (range 0–27). Higher SWLS scores indicate greater life satisfaction (range 5–35).
Results and Discussion
The researchers hypothesized that gratitude journaling would significantly reduce depression scores and increase life satisfaction compared to the control condition. The results are shown in the table.

Mean Pre- and Post-Intervention Scores by Condition

Measure | Gratitude Condition (Pre) | Gratitude Condition (Post) | Control Condition (Pre) | Control Condition (Post)
PHQ-9 (depression severity) | 9.4 | 6.1 | 9.2 | 8.7
SWLS (life satisfaction) | 18.3 | 23.7 | 18.1 | 19.0

Statistical analyses revealed that participants in the gratitude condition showed significantly greater reductions in depression and significantly greater increases in life satisfaction compared to the control group. The researchers concluded that a brief, low-cost gratitude journaling practice shows promise as a supplementary strategy for managing mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms, though they noted that longer follow-up studies are needed.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately identifies the research method as an experiment.
The study was an experiment because participants were randomly assigned to either the gratitude journaling condition or the control condition.
This is an experiment because the researchers manipulated the independent variable (type of journaling) and used random assignment.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of depression severity as used in the study.
rubric_criteria: The response states that depression severity was operationally defined as the participant's score on the PHQ-9 questionnaire, with higher scores indicating greater severity.
Depression was measured by the PHQ-9, a validated questionnaire, with scores ranging from 0 to 27.
The operational definition of depression severity is the PHQ-9 score completed at the start and end of the four-week intervention.

parts.prompt: Describe what the data in the table indicate about the change in life satisfaction scores for the gratitude journaling condition compared to the control condition.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately describes that the gratitude journaling group showed a larger increase in life satisfaction (from 18.3 to 23.7, an increase of 5.4 points) compared to the control group (from 18.1 to 19.0, an increase of only 0.9 points).
Life satisfaction improved much more in the gratitude journaling condition (an increase of 5.4 points) than in the control condition (an increase of 0.9 points).
Both groups started with similar life satisfaction scores, but after four weeks, the gratitude group's SWLS scores were approximately 4.7 points higher than the control group's post-scores.

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline the researchers followed in conducting this study.
rubric_criteria: The response identifies that participants with clinical diagnoses were excluded to protect them from potential harm (they needed more intensive treatment).
The response identifies that participants gave informed consent before enrolling in the study.

The response identifies that the researchers screened for depression level to ensure they did not work with severely symptomatic individuals who needed professional care (protection from harm / beneficence).

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable, using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
rubric_criteria: The response proposes a generalizability claim referencing a relevant population with specific evidence from the participant description.
The findings may generalize to adults in the 22–58 age range with mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms because that was the target population and inclusion criteria.
The findings may not generalize to people with severe depression or diagnosed disorders because these individuals were specifically excluded from the study.
The findings may have limited generalizability across cultures or to non-Western populations because the sample was predominantly White/European American and recruited through online advertisements.
parts.scoring_notes: If students claim the study is 'partly' generalizable, their evidence must support both a reason it is and is not generalizable.

parts.prompt: Explain how the concept of positive psychology relates to the rationale for this study.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately explains that positive psychology focuses on building strengths, positive emotions, and well-being rather than only treating mental illness, and that this study's gratitude journaling intervention is grounded in positive psychology by using a strengths-based approach (cultivating gratitude) to reduce depressive symptoms and improve life satisfaction.

Positive psychology emphasizes fostering positive emotions and well-being, and this study applies that approach by using gratitude journaling — a positive psychology intervention — to address mild depression.
The study reflects positive psychology principles by targeting the cultivation of gratitude (a positive emotion) rather than directly targeting the elimination of depressive symptoms.

parts.prompt: Explain how the role of dopamine could relate to the improvement in mood seen in the gratitude journaling condition.
rubric_criteria: The response accurately explains that dopamine is a neurotransmitter associated with pleasure, reward, and motivation, and that focusing on positive experiences through gratitude journaling may activate dopaminergic reward pathways in the brain, contributing to improved mood and reduced depression.
Gratitude journaling may increase dopamine activity in the brain's reward system, producing feelings of pleasure and positive affect that reduce depressive symptoms.
Dopamine, linked to the experience of reward and positive feeling, may be released when participants focus on positive events during gratitude journaling, helping explain the improvement in mood.
